mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XJ 3.0d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned AJD‑V6 3.0 engine typically costs £6,500–£9,500 supply only, with fitting adding £1,600–£2,200. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your XJ.

It's a capable and refined engine, but it has a known weakness — the crankshaft bearing design. With aggressive 5,000‑mile oil changes and careful monitoring, it can be reliable. Without that maintenance, it is one of the more failure‑prone modern JLR diesels.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the XJ 3.0d engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Crankshaft bearing fatigue (catastrophic failure risk), turbocharger contamination from oil system issues, and diesel injector clogging. The crank bearing is the defining issue for this engine.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an XJ 3.0d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. An X351 XJ is a £5,000–£18,000 vehicle — an £8,000 recon is 44–160% of the car's value. This sits at or above our 60% Jaguar scrap threshold, so evaluate condition carefully. A well‑maintained example with low miles justifies replacement; a tired, high‑mileage one may not.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my XJ 3.0d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The XJ 3.0d engine code is AJD‑V6 3.0 (306DT‑family). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the crankshaft bearing failure on an XJ 3.0d?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — early intervention is key. The single most effective prevention is 5,000‑mile oil changes with JLR‑spec 5W30 or 0W30 oil. Some specialists also recommend oil analysis from 80,000 miles to monitor bearing wear. At the first sign of cold‑start knock, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Crankshaft Bearing Failure (Catastrophic)
problems.symptoms: Cold‑start knock or rumble, metallic tapping that fades as the engine warms up, oil pressure warning light, sudden engine seizure.
problems.repairCost: N/A — cannot be repaired; bottom‑end rebuild or replacement required

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear cold knock, stop driving and book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: This is the single most important issue on the AJD‑V6 — the crankshaft bearing design has a fatigue life. The key defence is aggressive oil change intervals: 5,000 miles max, not the factory 15,000‑mile schedule. If you hear a rumble or knock on cold start that fades as the engine warms up, have the oil pressure checked and the bearings inspected immediately. A bottom‑end rebuild (£5,500–£7,000) is cheaper than a full engine replacement, but only if caught early.

problems.title: Oil Pump / Turbo Contamination
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, turbo whistle or whine, oil pressure warning, excessive exhaust smoke.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before turbo disintegrates)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if turbo noise or smoke appears

problems.recommendation: The AJD‑V6's turbocharger is vulnerable to oil contamination from the crankcase ventilation system. Regular oil changes with the correct 5W30 or 0W30 JLR‑spec oil are essential — and insist on OEM or OE‑branded oil filters. If you notice a whistle, whine, or loss of power, have the turbo inspected without delay.

problems.title: Diesel Injector Failure
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, white or black smoke, loss of power, increased fuel consumption, engine rattle.
problems.recommendation: Ford‑sourced injectors are common across the AJD‑V6 and can clog over time, especially if fuel filter changes are skipped. A periodic injector clean and using premium diesel can reduce the risk. If you notice rough running or smoke, have the injectors tested — replacing a full set is far cheaper than the collateral damage they can cause.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This XJ 3.0d? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented 5,000‑mile oil change history (not 15,000‑mile factory schedule)
* No cold‑start knock or rumble — walk away if you hear anything unusual
* Full service history with JLR‑spec oil and genuine filters
avoidIf: * Cold‑start knock or rumble — this is the early warning sign of bearing failure
* No record of regular oil changes — AJD‑V6s are unforgiving of neglect
* Engine oil pressure warning light — could indicate pump or bearing issues
* Turbo whistle or whine without service history — contamination may have occurred

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The AJD‑V6 3.0 is a torquey, refined engine that powers the XJ well. But it has a known Achilles heel — the crankshaft bearing design. Buy only on documented evidence of 5,000‑mile oil changes, and never ignore a cold‑start knock. A well‑maintained example is a reliable luxury saloon; a neglected one is a £7,000–£10,000 bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare XJ 3.0d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar XJ 3.0d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rumble or knock that fades as the engine warms — crank bearing warning sign
* Service History — including engine era (AJD‑V6 3.0) and confirmed 5,000‑mile oil change intervals
* Oil Clarity — Check the dipstick; metallic glitter indicates bearing wear
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any whistle, whine, or lag?
* DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear on the DPF and turbo
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
